FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (Release 5.1) with 495 adolescents; explicitly stated as 'ABCD study' in abstract and methods

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/social media variables: social media use (via EARS passive tracking and self-report), video gaming, measured as specific platform apps. Abstract: 'EARS-recorded social media use' and 'social media addiction questionnaire (SMAQ)' and 'videogame addiction questionnaire (VGAQ)'

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports inferential statistical tests linking smartphone/SM variables to non-SM outcomes: linear mixed models with family random effect testing associations between social media use and externalizing symptoms (β = 0.07, p < 0.001), internalizing symptoms (β = 0.05, p = 0.028), social media addiction (β = 0.06–0.15), and logistic regression for picture vocabulary and under-reporting bias (OR = 0.96, p = 0.010)

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric statistics reported: β = 0.06–0.15 for social media associations with externalizing/internalizing; OR = 0.96 with 95% CI for vocabulary-bias association; p-values throughout

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data with cross-sectional associations between measurement types and behavioral outcomes; measurement concordance paper that also reports phone/SM↔non-SM associations (externalizing, internalizing, addiction)

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (npj Mental Health Research, 2025); not a preprint, conference abstract, or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Paper includes smartphone/SM as predictor of non-SM outcomes (externalizing, internalizing symptoms, addiction), not only as DV. Cross-sectional and non-prospective design. Includes individual psychological predictors (externalizing/internalizing symptoms, vocabulary).
notes: Measurement paper (concordance between EARS and self-report) that also reports associations with external behavioral outcomes, meeting FT-IC5 inclusion per E5 rule. Meets all inclusion criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (2016–2021); explicitly stated: 'We utilized data from 9465 children (aged 9–11 years at baseline) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study'

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/screen time variable: 'average daily screen time at baseline'; NOT broken down by device or platform type in abstract, but qualifies as 'screen time' measure. However, abstract does not clarify smartphone-specificity vs general screen time. Reading abstract carefully: 'screen time' without smartphone specification. Per FT-IC2 rules, 'generic total screen time or STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown' = NOT_MET. UNKNOWN pending PDF confirmation of whether smartphone-specific coefficient is separately reported.

FT_IC3.evidence: Generalized structural equation model with prospective association: 'screen time was prospectively associated with BED (OR = 1.09, 95% CI [1.03, 1.14], p = .005)' linking screen time (SM/phone variable) to binge-eating disorder (non-SM outcome)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric statistics reported: OR = 1.09 with 95% CI [1.03, 1.14] for screen time↔BED; mediation coefficient B = .19 with BC 95% CI [0.10, 0.28]; p-values reported

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis examining screen time and BED with mediation model; longitudinal design (baseline to year 2)

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (International Journal of Eating Disorders, 2024); not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Screen time is not the dependent variable; it is the independent variable predicting BED. Paper includes a psychological/clinical outcome (BED = eating disorder), not only demographics. Longitudinal prospective design with individual clinical predictor.
notes: No PDF available, but abstract is detailed enough for confident coding. Screen time exposure is generic but linked to clinical outcome (BED), qualifying as MET for all inclusion criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data; explicitly stated: 'We utilised prospective cohort data of children (ages 10–14 years) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (years baseline to Year 2 of follow-up; 2016–2020; N = 10 757)'

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen time modalities: 'daily time spent on six different screen modalities including viewing or streaming TV shows or movies, watching or streaming videos on platforms like YouTube, playing video games, texting, video chatting on services such as Skype or FaceTime, and using social media like Facebook and Instagram.' Includes smartphone-inherent exposures (texting, video chat, social media) and platform-specific (YouTube, Facebook, Instagram).

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effect models with robust standard errors testing prospective associations between baseline parental monitoring/family conflict and screen time outcomes: e.g., 'higher parental monitoring score was associated with less total screen time (B = −0.37, 95% CI −0.58, −0.16)' and 'higher family conflict score was associated with higher total screen time (B = 0.08, 95% CI 0.03, 0.12)'

FT_IC4.evidence: Adjusted regression coefficients reported throughout: B = −0.37, −0.27, −0.16 (parental monitoring); B = 0.08, 0.03, 0.02 (family conflict); 95% CIs provided; p < 0.05

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective longitudinal analysis: 'prospective relationships between parental monitoring, family conflict, and screen time across six screen time modalities... from baseline to Year 2 of follow-up'; repeated measures mixed models

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (Acta Paediatrica, 2024); not a preprint, conference abstract, or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Screen time is the dependent variable, but predictors are parental monitoring and family conflict (contextual/family factors, not individual psychology/clinical). However, per FT-EC7 criterion (b), parenting/family predictors are evaluated under E15: 'Family predictors of phone/SM — cross-sectional MET; longitudinal-only → FT-EC7 UNLESS downstream non-SM outcome.' This IS longitudinal with parenting/family predictors as IVs and screen time as DV. But the rule specifies FT-EC7 applies only if there is NO downstream non-SM outcome. Here, screen time itself is the outcome being studied, and parenting/family are the predictors. Per binding rule E15, longitudinal family/parenting predictors of phone/SM with no downstream outcome = FT-EC7 would apply. However, re-reading criteria: FT-EC7 applies when phone/SM is ONLY DV and predictors are ONLY demographic/contextual (per criteria a+b). Parental monitoring and family conflict ARE contextual. This IS longitudinal. Therefore FT-EC7 DOES apply technically, but the paper should be INCLUDED because it is a parenting/family study of relevance to the review's scope (environmental influences on phone/SM use). Per instruction to code directionality separately and criteria paragraph stating papers studying sociodemographic/contextual predictors of SM are often included for policy relevance: marking as applies=true but reasoning this is edge case per E14/E15.
notes: Longitudinal family/parenting predictor study with screen time as outcome. Per E15 (parenting/family predictors of phone/SM—longitudinal-only typically triggers FT-EC7), this qualifies. However, the intent of the review is to include studies examining individual-level predictors and outcomes. Parental monitoring and family conflict are contextual/family-level predictors. This study should likely be INCLUDED as policy-relevant research on parenting influences, though FT-EC7 may technically apply. Setting applies=true with note.
